text: ERBALUNGA, a hamlet and small port in the island of Corsica, in the cant. and com. of Brando, on the E coast of the island. Pop. 297.
ERBAUT, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, 10 m. SW of Soignies. Pop. 440.
ERBAYN (JEBEL), a mountain of Syria, in the pash. of Damascus, S of Hamah, in N lat. 35° 8', E long. 36° 36′.

ERBE, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. and 15 m. SSW of Verona, district and 3 m. SW of Isola-della-Scala. Pop. 1,500. ERBENDORF, EBENDORF, or ARNDORF, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Upper Franconia, 26 m. ESE of Bayreuth, on the r. bank of the Waldnaab. Pop. 1,400. It has 2 churches, a Catholic and a Lutheran; and possesses manufactories of linen, and several tanneries. It was destroyed by fire in 1771.
ERBENHEIM, a village of the duchy of Nassau, SE of Weisbaden.

ERBESBUDESHEIM, a village of the grandduchy of Hessen, NW of Alzey. Pop. 800. ERBIL, ARBEL [Wolff], ARBILE [Fraser], or Arbeles, a town of Turkey in Asia, in the pash. of Bagdad, 60 m. ESE of Mosul. Pop. 10,000, chiefly Kurds. It is situated in a fertile plain on the Indoi- Su, backed by the Carduchian mountains. It contains a bath, caravanserais, and bazaars; and is defended by a fort, which stands on a high flat summit about 150 ft. in height. Hares, antelopes, and kattahs or desert-partridges, abound on the surrounding plain.—This town occupies the site of the ancient Arbela, famous for the decisive victory which Alexander here gained over Darius. The classic reader will find a few imperfect but interesting notes on the topography of this locality, with reference to the descriptions of this battle, in Mr. Rich's 2d vol., App. No. 2. It is identified by the Chaldean Christians with the Rehoboth of Genesis x. 11.
ERBISDORF, a village of Saxony, in the circle of the Erzgebirge, bail. and 4 m. SSW of Freyberg, and 24 m. SW of Dresden. Pop. 425. It has a rich mine of silver.
ERBISOEUL, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, 11 m. SW of Soignies. Pop. 650.
ERBISTOCK, a parish, partly in Denbighshire, partly in Flintshire, 5 m. NW of Ellesmere, on the Dee. Pop. 423.
ERBLON (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. of Rennes. Pop. 1,350.
ERBRAY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Loire - Inferieure, cant. of Saint - Julien - de - Vouvantes, 5 m. SE of Chateaubriant. Pop. 2,120. It has lime-kilns, and quarries of grey marble.
ERBREE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. of Vitré. Pop. 1,667.
ERCALL MAGNA, or HIGH MAGNA, a parish of Shropshire, 6 m. NW of Wellington, E of the Roden. Area 11,780 acres. Pop. 1,999.
ERCE, a commune and town of France, in the prov. of Ariège, cant. of Oust, 13 m. SE of Saint- Girons, on the r. bank of the Garbet. Pop. in 1841, 3,855. It has mines of iron and tin.
ERCE'-EN-LAME'E, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, cant. of Bain, 33 m. NE of Redon, on the l. bank of the Bruc. Pop. in 1841, 3,040.
ERCE'-PRE'S-LIFFRE', a commune of France, in the dep. of Ille-et-Vilaine, cant of Liffre. Pop. 1,589.
ERCHEK, a village of Asiatic Turkey, in the pash. of Van, 22 m. NE of Van, and 2 m. from the E side of Lake Erchekjun, a fine sheet of water from 12 to 15 m. in length, and 8 or 9 m. in width.
ERCHEUX, a commune of France, in the dep. of | the Somme, cant. of Roye. Pop. 1,014.
ERCHIE, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Otranto, district and 22 m. SW of Brindisi.
ERCHINA, a mountain of Spain, in the prov. of Guipuzcoa, near Cestona. It affords excellent pasturage; and has quarries of slate and jasper.

ERCOLE (Porto D'), a small port on the coast of Tuscany, at the E base of Monte Argentaro, 15 m. WNW of the mouth of the Fiore. The bay, on the W side of which the port stands, is open to all winds from the S, especially to the SE.

ERCSI, or Ercse'ny, a village of Hungary, in the comitat and to the E of Stuhlweissenburg, on an arm of the Danube, on which it has a steam-boat station. Pop. 3,296. It has extensive manufactories of coleseed-oil, a distillery of brandy, and a manufactory of vinegar. Madder and teasel are cultivated in large quantities in the environs.
ERDBERG, a town of Moravia, in the circle and 12 m. ESE of Znaim, on the l. bank of the Taya, near the frontier of Austria. Pop. 1,086.
ERDBORN, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of Saxony, circle of Merseburg, NW of Schraplau. Pop. 557.
ERDEK. See ARTAKI.

ERDENI-TCHAO, a town of Mongolia, in the territory of the Khalkas, 800 m. NW of Pekin, on the Orchon. It is now to a great extent in ruins. It is supposed by Fisher to occupy the site of the Kara-kum, cap. of the empire Jenghiz-khan. The situation of that town is however placed by Anville 210 m. further to the SE.

ERDER, a village of the principality of Lippe- Detmold, 2 m. WNW of Varenholz, on the l. bank of the Weser. It contains about 55 houses, and a custom-house. It is the principal entrepot for the trade of the principality.
ERDEVEN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Morbihan, cant. of Belz. Pop. in 1841, 3,002.

ERDHAUSEN, a village of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Schwarzwald, on the Nagold, SE of Bernek. ERDHOLMS, EARTH-HOLMS, or ERTHOLMEN, a small group of rocky islands in the Baltic, belonging to Denmark, and situated about 61 m. to the NE of Bornholm. They are 4 in number; but 2 only, Christiansöe and Fredericksholm, are inhabited. The former is about 1,200 ft. long, and 400 ft. broad; and has an area of 500,000 sq. ft. About 400 of the pop. of the group reside on it. Fredericksholm is only 900 ft. long, and about 450 ft. broad. Between these two is a harbour about 800 ft. in length, and nearly 200 ft. in width, affording safe anchorage for 40 or 50 vessels; and having deep water, with a double entrance: the S opening, however, is not accessible to vessels drawing above 12 ft. water. Between the two entrances, the two islands approach so near to each other that they are united by a floating-bridge 80 ft. long. Pilots are always in readiness here, for vessels navigating the Baltic. There is a lighthouse on Christiansöe rock, in N lat. 55° 19', E long. 15° 12', elevated 92 ft. above the level of the sea. Batteries and towers, mounting in all 90 guns, are erected on these islands; and the garrison, in time of peace, is about 90 men. The other two islands, It is enclosed by a wall flanked with towers; and Græsholm and Tat, are uninhabited, but are covered with geese which yield a highly prized down, valued at 8s. 6d. per lb. on the spot. The whole are surrounded with rocks and sand-banks, on which salmon, eels, and other kinds of fish are procured. A boat goes twice a-week for provisions to Gudhjelm.

ERDING, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Upper Bavaria, on the l. bank of the Semt, 10 m. ESE of Freising, and 20 m. NE of Munich. Pop. 1,823. hair-cordage, wax, and timber. Its chief imports are ERE It has 4 churches, and several hospitals; and possesses manufactories of linen, several tanneries and forges, and a saw-mill.
ERDINGTON, a hamlet in the p. of Aston, Warwickshire. Pop. 2,579.
ERDMANNSDORF, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of Silesia, circle of Hirschberg. China-clay, and rock-crystal, are found in the environs.
ERDMANNSHAUSEN, a village of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Neckar, 2 m. E of Marbach. Pop. 1,065.

ERDOD, a town of Hungary, in the comitat and 10 m. S of Szathmar, at the foot of one of the ramifications of the mountains of Transylvania. It has a castle, now in ruins; and possesses several glassworks.—Also a town of Hungarian Slavonia, in the comitat of Verocze, on the Danube, 5 m. ESE of the confluence of the Drave.
ERDOD-SZADA, or ARDO-SZADA, a town of Hungary, in the comitat and 27 m. SE of Szathmar, in a woody district near the l. bank of the Szamos. It has a Greek church.
ERDO-KOVESD, a village of Hungary, in the comitat of Heves, 15 m. NW of Eger. It is noted for its manufactories of potash.

ERDRE, a river of France, which takes its rise in the dep. of the Maine-et-Loire, 8 m. E of Cande; runs WSW past that town; enters the dep. of the Loire-Inferieure; bends S; and unites with the Loire on the r. bank, at Nantes, after a course of about 63 m. It is navigable to Nort, where it is joined by the Nantes and Brest canal. Its only affluent of any importance is the Croissel.
EREAC, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Cotes-du-Nord, cant. of Broons. Pop. 1,269.

EREBUS (Mount), a volcanic mountain in the newly discovered Antarctic continent of Victoria Land, in S lat. 77° 31', E long. 167° 1'. It attains an alt. of 12,367 ft. above sea-level, and is covered with ice and snow from its base to its summit, from which a dense column of black smoke was continually ascending while Sir James C. Ross's expedition remained in sight; whenever the smoke cleared away, a bright red flame, filling the mouth of the crater, was perceptible.

EREGUP ISLANDS, a group of coral reefs in the Radack chain, intersected by the parallel of 9° 5' N lat., and the meridian of 189° 45' W long. They are separated by a narrow channel from Otdia on the N. The length of the reef is 24 m.; its general breadth 4 m.

EREKLI, a town of Turkey in Europe, in Rumelia, in the sanjak and 95 m. SSE of Adrianople, and 55 m. W of Constantinople, on the sea of Marmara, on which it has a double port. Its pop. has been estimated at 7,000; but other and perhaps more credible authorities, state that a few fishermen form now its only inhabitants. It occupies the site of the ancient Perinthus.

EREKLI, or BENDER-EREGLI, a town of Turkey in Asia, in Anatolia, in the sanj. and 45 m. NNW of Boli, and 130 m. ENE of Constantinople, on a gulf of the Black sea, enclosed on the N by a small peninsula, anciently named Acherusia chersonesus, in N lat. 41° 15' 30", E long. 31° 25'. Pop. 5,000, chiefly Turks. contains a castle, 5 mosques, 2 khans, 2 public baths, about 200 shops, and numerous remains of the ancient Heraclea, of which it occupies the site. The remains of the walls of the ancient city may still be traced throughout their outline. It has a good port, and building-docks; and is defended by 4 batteries of from 4 to 6 guns each. It possesses some linen manufactories, and carries on an active export trade in silk, linen, cabans or Zagora cloaks, shawls, coffee, sugar, rice, tobacco, iron, and tin. The country in the immediate vicinity is of a marshy nature, and is traversed by an ancient causeway. The formations around E. consist of igneous and sedimentary rocks. EREKLI, ERAKLI, EREGLI, or ERCLE, a considerable town of Turkey in Asia, in Caramania, in the sanj. and 78 m. ESE of Konieh, to the E of a small lake. It contains about 800 houses, generally built of sun-dried brick, and has a well-built stone mosque. It is on the route of the caravan from Constantinople to Damascus. The environs are extremely luxuriant, and afford large quantities of cherries and other fruit, and madder. The latter article is often shipped at Tarsus for the European markets. This town was the Archelais of the ancients.
EREMBODEGEM, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of E. Flanders, on the Dendre. Pop. 3,127.

EREMEISKIE, a chain of mountains of considerable height in Independent Tartary, in the steppe which extends between the Ural and Irtish. It is 36 m. in length, and about 2 m. in breadth. It is inhabited by the Kirghiz; and its forests form the retreat of large numbers of wild animals.
EREMITAGE, a town of Denmark, in the island of Sieland, 7 m. N of Copenhagen.

ERESMA, a river of Spain, in Old Castile, which takes its rise 8 m. S of St. Ildefonse, in the prov. of Segovia; passes near that town; receives on the l. the Moros and Boltoya; enters the prov. of Valladolid; and, after a course in a generally NNW direction, unites with the Adaja on the r. bank.
ERETZ, a small fortified town in Georgia, in the prov. of Shirvan, 60 m. NNW of Nova-Shamaka, near the l. bank of the Kur. Silk is extensively cultivated in the environs.
ERE'ZE'E, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Luxemburg, 12 m. NE of Marche, on the r. bank of the Aisne. Pop. 690.
ERFA, or ERF, a village of Bavaria, in the circle of Lower Franconia, on the Mayn, near Miltenberg.
ERFDE, a town of Denmark, in the duchy of Sleswig, 12 m. ESE of Friederickstadt. Pop. 800.

ERFELDEN, or ERFELD, a village of Hesse- Darmstadt, in the prov. of Starkenburg, on the r. bank of the Rhine, 8 m. WSW of Darmstadt. Pop. 673. On the bank of the river is a column of stone surmounted by the figure of a lion, erected by Gustavus Adolphus in memory of his passage of the Rhine at that place on the 6th Dec., 1631.

ERFFT, or ERFT, a river of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, which has its source in the mountains of Eifel, in the S part of the regency of Cologne, 5 m. SW of Rheinbach; runs NW past Mekenheim, Bedburg, and Caster; enters the regency of Dusseldorf, passes Grevenbroich, where it becomes navigable, and, after a total and very sinuous course of about 66 m., falls into the Rhine at Grimlighausen, 9 m. below Grevenbroich, and 4 m. S of Dusseldorf. Its principal affluents are the Kassel and the Nassen. Its waters abound with fish.

ERFURT, an administrative subdivision of Prussian Saxony, consisting of the old principality of E., the bails. of Langensalza and Weissensee, the grafschaft of Holmstein, the free royal cities of Mühlhausen and Nordhausen, the grafschaft of Henneberg, the bail of Zeigenrück, and some other small districts. It has some isolated districts; but the main portion of the regency lies between Hanover and Brunswick on the N; the regency of Merseburg on the E.; Saxe-Weimar, Schwarzburg-Rudolstadt, and Gotha on the S; and Hesse-Cassel on the W. It has a total area of 62 German, or 1,316 English sq. Its pop. in 1816 was 234,427; in 1837, 305,888, m. of whom 216,825 were Protestants and 87,733 Ca- ERG tholics; and in 1842, 330,000. The principal rivers intersecting this regency are the Unstrutt, the Gera, the Werra, Salza, Wipper, and Saale. About one-half of the surface is arable; and the principal productions are grain, flax, tobacco, and hops. Iron, lead, copper, and gypsum are wrought.—The regency is divided into 9 circles: viz. Nordhausen, Warbis, Heiligenstadt, Mühlhausen, Langen-Salje, Weissensee, Erfurt, Ziegenrück, and Schleusingen. Its principal towns are Erfurt, Sömmerda, Langensalza, Weissensee, Bleichrode, Mühlhausen, Nordhausen, Schlensingen, and Suhl.

ERFURT, the cap. of the above regency, is situated on the Gera, a tributary of the Unstrutt, in N lat. 50° 48′ 45″, E long. 11° 2′ 26″, 18 m. direct distance W of Weimar, and 20 m. direct distance E by N of Gotha, on the great Thuringian line of railroad, and at an alt. of 633 ft. above sea-level. It is an ill-built town, but is fortified, and has two citadels, the Petersberg and the Cyriaksburg, and possesses several public buildings, a cathedral, numerous churches, and several public libraries. Its university, established in 1389, was suppressed in 1816. Its present pop., including the garrison, is about 28,000. In the 16th cent. it was a place of considerably greater commercial importance, and formed an entrepot between Upper and Lower Germany; but it has still active manufactories of woollens, linens, ribands, leather, shoes, soap, pipes, vermicelli, and oil; and conducts a considerable trade in grain and seeds, the produce of the adjacent territory, which is well-cultivated and fertile.—This town, with about 200 sq. m. of surrounding country, belonged to the electors of Mentz, who governed by a deputy-bailiff. In 1803 it was attached to Prussia by way of indemnity for certain losses of territory. After the disastrous battle of Jena, a corps of 14,000 Prussians retreated into E., but, with a park of artillery of 120 guns, were captured by the French next day. In 1808, a memorable interview took place here betwixt Napoleon and Alexander, attended by brilliant suites; and to the possession of E. in October 1813, the French army was mainly indebted for its escape after the fatal battle of Leipsic. E. has recently attracted political notice, as being the place chosen for the sittings of what was called 'the Princely college,' the object of which was the reconstruction of the Germanic federacy on a broader and more national basis. With this view, and after the failure of the Frankfort conferences held with the same object, a league was formed, on the 26th of May, 1849, between Prussia, Saxony, and Hanover, which was subsequently joined by most of the smaller states; and a constitutional union was adopted, which it was hoped would "satisfy the great national idea of Germany." The history and fate of this measure are traced in our historical notice of GERMANY.
ERG, or AERGEREN, a river of Switzerland, in the cant. of Freyburg, which descends from the N side of Mount Berra, and unites with the Saane, 4 m. SSW of Freyburg.
ERGENT. See BERATINO.

ERGENZINGEN, a town of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Schwarzwald, bail. and 6 m. WNW of Rottemburg. Pop. in 1840, 1,537. Wool-spinning and the manufacture of wooden-ware form the chief branches of local industry.

ERGETU, or Jugurtai, a river of Russia in Asia, in the gov. of Omsk, which descends from the mountains of Kaga-Kara-Bulak, runs S, and throws itself into the Balkash-Nor, or Lake Tengiz, after a course of about 216 m,

ERGHEN, or Ergono, a river of Turkistan, which takes its rise in the Tsoungling or Kara-Korum chain, under the name of the Yarkand or Yarkiang river; runs first NW and then bends NE, passing Yarkand; and after crossing the 40th parallel is joined by the Kashgar and Aksu on the l., and on the r. by the Khoten. Thence it takes the name of Tarim to the junction of the Mussur-Bai, when it assumes the appellation of the Erghen, and after receiving the Kaidon river, the outlet of the Bosteng or Boston-Nor, discharges itself into the Lob-Nor, after a total course of 1,050 m.
ERGIK-TARGAK-TAIGAN, an extensive mountain-range in the chain of the Altai, in Central Asia, stretching between 52° and 53° N lat., and from 94° to 98° E long.

ERGIR-KASTRI. See ARGIRO-KASTRO. ERGOLZ, ERGELITZ, or ERGEZ, a river of Switzerland, in the cant. of Basel, which has its rise at the foot of the Schafmatt, runs E to Liestall, where it receives the Fanke, and thence directs its course N to the Rhine, which it joins at Augst-Basel, after a total course of 18 m.
ERGSTE, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of Westphalia, 23 m. W of Arensberg. Pop. 674. It has a manufactory of nails.
ERGU, a small town of Tibet, 65 m. SE of Budakshan, and on one of the head-streams of the river of that name.
ERGUE'-ARMEL, a commune of France, in the dep. of Finistère, cant. of Quimper. Pop. 1,641.
ERGUE'-GABE'RIC, a commune of France, in the dep. of Finistère, cant. of Quimper. Pop. 2,012.
ERGUEL. See SAINT-IMIER.
ERGUN. See AMUR.

ERIBOLL (Loch), an arm of the Atlantic ocean, running into the p. of Durness, Sutherlandshire. It is about 11 m. in length, from 1 to 3 m. in breadth, and varying in depth from 15 to 60 fathoms. At Camisendunbay, about 7 m. from its entrance, is excellent anchorage, and a ferry, 2 m. broad. Its E shore, from the Whiten-head southwards, presents a series of caves and arches "the most extensive and extraordinary," according to Macculloch, "on any part of the Scottish coast." At its upper end is some fine Alpine scenery, amongst mountains of quartz and grey slate, in which Benhope is conspicuous. Near the entrance of this loch is the island of Hoan, about 1 m. in length and a 1 m. in breadth; and a little above Camisendunbay-ferry is another island, of nearly the same dimensions, called Choarie.
ERICEIRA, a town of Portugal, in the prov. of Estremadura, in the comarca and 11 m. SW of Torres-Vedras, 26 m. NW of Lisbon, on a small gulf. Pop. 2,550, chiefly connected with fisheries.
ERICH, an ancient town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bundelcund, on the r. bank of the Betwa, 8 m. E of the town of that name, and 183 m. W of Allahabad.
ERICHSHAGEN, a town of Hanover, in the principality of Kalenberg, 31 m. NW of Hanover. Pop. 442.

ERICHT, or EROCHD (LOCH), a lake partly in the p. of Fortingall, co. of Perth, and partly in the p. of Laggan, co. of Inverness. It is about 16 m. in length, and 1 m. in breadth. According to ancient tradition, the district now covered by the waters of this lake was formerly dry, and constituted an entire p. called Feadail, which, with its inhabitants, was overwhelmed in one night, by the sudden bursting of an immense body of subterranean water. Its waters are emptied into Loch-Rannoch, which lies about 5 m. to the S. whence they flow, through Loch-Tummel, into the river Tay. There is no road to Loch-Ericht; but it may be visited at its S extremity, either from the head of Loch-Rannoch, to which there is a road along that lake, or from the inn at Dalnacardoch on the Great North road. From either of these | The goods principally sent by this conveyance are timber, staves, ERI places a huge extent of bog, muir, moss, and mountain has to be traversed: but by approaching from Loch-Rannoch, a much less portion of this sort of travelling is necessary than if the visitor attempts to cross the mountains from Dalnacardoch.

ERICHT, a stream which issues from the S end of the above lake, and after a course of a few miles falls into Loch-Rannoch.—Also a river in the E of Perthshire, formed by the junction of the Airdle and the Shee in the p. of Blairgowrie, which it crosses, and flowing in a SE direction, falls into the Isla opposite Balbrogy, in the p. of Cupar-Angus. Its channel is rocky, and its stream rapid and turbulent. The scenery on its banks is in many places singularly romantic. The entire course of the river does not exceed 17 m.

ERIE, a county of the state of New York, U. S., comprising an area of 876 sq. m., bounded on the W by Lake Erie and Niagara river, and on the S by Cattaraugus river, and drained by Buffalo, Cayuga, Cazenove, Seneca, Ellicott's, Cauquaga, and Tonewanda creeks. The Erie canal, which communicates with Lake Erie by means of an extensive basin formed by the pier at Black Rock, runs through the co.; and a railroad connects the capital, Buffalo, with Niagara falls. The surface is undulating in the N, and in the S rises to considerable hills. Pop. in 1830, 35,710; in 1840, 62,465.—Also a co. in the NW part of the state of Pennsylvania, containing a superficies of 720 sq. m., bounded on the NW by Lake Erie, and watered by Coneaut, Elk, Walnut, and other creeks, affluents of Lake Erie and French-creek, which flow into Alleghany river. Its surface is undulating, and its soil a compound of sandy loam, gravel, and clay. Pop. in 1830, 17,027; in 1840, 31,344. Its cap., Erie, which is a port of entry, is beautifully situated on Presque-Isle bay, and has one of the best harbours on the lake. Pop. 3,412.— Also a co. in the N part of Ohio, skirting Loch Erie on the N. It has an area of 150 sq. m., and is watered by Huron and Vermillion rivers. Pop. 12,599. Its cap. is Huron.—Also a v. in Greene co., in the state of Alabama, 48 m. S by W of Tuscaloosa.— Also a v. in Ottawa co., in the state of Ohio, 16 m. N of Lower Sandusky, on the N side of Portage river. Pop. 200.—Also a v. in Monroe co., in Michigan, 47 m. SW of Detroit. Pop. 852.

ERIE CANAL, the most important of the great lines of artificial navigation pervading the United States. The following account of this magnificent work is abridged from Mr. Mackay's Western World. This canal unites the sea-board at New York with the region bordering the lakes at Buffalo. Its E extremity is at Albany, where it joins the Hudson, 160 m. above New York. Thence it proceeds W along the valley of the Mohawk, passing through or by the towns of Schenectady, Canojoharie, Little-Falls, Utica, Rome, Syracuse, and Palmyra, crossing the Genessee valley at Rochester, and then proceeding W by Lockport to Buffalo. Its entire length is about 370 m. It puts the city of New York in connection with the lakes far above the rapids of the St. Lawrence, and beyond the falls of Niagara; in other words, it opens up to New York the trade and traffic of the vast basin in which the lakes lie. It also connects at two different points, though by a circuitous route, the sea-board with the Mississippi valley; the Genessee valley canal uniting it at Rochester with the Alleghany river in Pennsylvania, which is one of the parents of the Ohio; and the great Ohio canal connecting Lake Erie, in which the Erie canal terminates, with the Ohio river. From Erie to the Seneca river is a descent of 194 ft. in which are 25 locks; from thence to Rome it rises 48 ft. by 6 locks; and from thence to the Hudson the descent is 413 ft. by 46 locks. Its middle section, or summit level, runs along a plain of 691 m. This majestic work was planned and executed by De Witt Clinton, governor of New York, in 1817 and 1822. It was begun on 4th July 1817, and finished in October 1825. Its cost, as reported by the canal board of the state of New York, in Feb., 1837, was 7,143,790 dollars. In its original dimensions the canal was 40 ft. wide at the top, 28 ft. at the bottom, and 4 ft. deep: such, however, has been the increase of the traffic upon it, that, in order to accommodate it, the canal has of late years been doubled in size. This enlargement, which involved a much greater outlay than that required for the original construction of the canal, is not yet completed throughout, but is steadily progressing. flour, wheat, butter, stone, iron, and potashes. The navigation of the E. canal from Buffalo to Albany occupies 8 days. See article BUFFALO. The success of the New York system, of which the E. canal is the great feature, is indicated by the surplus revenues now derived from it. The aggregate cost of the canals was about 31,000,000 dollars, the average interest payable upon which is 51 per cent. The net revenue from all the state canals, after deducting the cost of collection and of superintendence, is upwards of 2,000,000 d., nearly equal to 7 per cent. upon the whole cost, or 11 per cent. beyond the average rate of interest payable upon it. The new constitution adopted by New York in defraying the expenses of civil government in the state. The Pennsylvanians, in constructing their great canal, which pursues a line almost parallel to that of the Erie canal, had a double object in view—that of facilitating their own internal, particularly their mineral, trade, and of creating a rival to the New York canal within the limits of Pennsylvania. It interferes but little, however, with the traffic of the E. canal.—The following table shows the time of opening the E. canal at Buffalo and at Albany, in the several years mentioned: at Buffalo April 21 at Albany

ERIE (LAKE), one of the five great lakes of N. America, lying between the frontiers of Canada and the United States, and comprised in the middle portion of the basin of the St. Lawrence, on the N side of the hilly country in which the head-waters of the Ohio originate, and on the S edge of the fertile peninsula enclosed by Lakes Huron, St. Clair, Erie, Ontario, and Simcoe. It lies between the parallels of 41° 22' and 42° 52' N; and between 78° 16' and 82° W long.; having on the N Upper Canada, and on the S and E the states of Ohio, Pennsylvania, and New York. Its shape is elliptical; measuring in length from SW to NE about 231 m.; its breadth, while averaging 38 m., varies from 10 m. to about 631 m. in its centre, at Port-Talbot, between the parallels of 41° 10' and 42° 3'. [Bouchette.] Its area is estimated by Darby at 8,030 sq. m. It receives, near its W extremity, the waters of lakes St. Clair and Huron, and of the upper lakes, by the deep navigable strait, 25 m. in length, called the Detroit river; its own surplus water is conveyed to Lake Ontario by means of the Niagara, celebrated for its stupendous waterfall, which issues from its NE end, and crossing with a general N by W course the intervening isthmus 26 m. broad, enters Lake Ontario, on the SW side, 46 m. from its head. See NIAGARA. The mean height of Lake E. above the level of the Hudson river at Albany, is estimated at 565 ft.; being about 52 ft. below that of lakes Michigan and Huron, and 322 ft. above that of Ontario. The immense declivity which lies between lakes E. and Ontario, occasions both the falls of Niagara and those of Cheneseco. Its depth, which is less than that of any of the other great lakes of the St. Lawrence basin, is no where more than 300 ft., and in most parts is considerably under 150 ft. It is also said to be gradually becoming shallower. A Buffalo journal asserts that Long Point had in 3 years gained 3 m. on the water; and that the land is also rapidly gaining along the S shore. The bottom of Lake E. appears to be composed of an alluvial deposit of sand and mud, resting on secondary schistose sandstone. The shores describe large and regular but shallow curvatures. The Canadian or N shore is rocky and dangerous; the opposite one has also long lines of rock; but runs out shoal for 2 or 3 m. in various places, and except at either extremity, none of its shore-harbours affords a steady entrance of 7 ft. water. In addition to other impediments to navigation, a current, not perceptible in the other great lakes of the St. Lawrence system, sets constantly W in Lake E.; and NW or SW winds continually pre- ERI vail upon it. It is rendered turbid in a gale of wind by the sand and mud washed from the bottom. In consequence of its shallowness, moreover, a part is frozen over every winter, and traffic on it is consequently obstructed by ice in spring, even for some weeks after the navigation of the other lakes is open and unimpeded. The following table shows the period of opening the navigation of Lake E. at Buffalo. There are several groups of small islands in the lake, amounting altogether to 26, all of which excepting 2 are at its S end. Point Pelé island is the largest, being <math>8\frac{3}{4}</math> m. long, and <math>4\frac{1}{2}</math> m. broad. It lies within the British line, 16 m. from the N shore, and 10 m. from the S. Cunningham island, belonging to the United States, 3½ m. NE of Sandusky peninsula, into the lake, the principal of which are the N. and S. Forelands, and Landguard point. The barrier which contains the lake is so low that a rise of 6 ft. in its waters would inundate some millions of acres on the N and W border; and a further rise of 6 or 8 ft. would probably precipitate a flood of waters over the state of Illinois. Except the Detroit, Lake E. receives few rivers of any consequence; and all of them, without exception, have bars at their mouths. The river Detroit enters it by a very wide mouth, but without occasioning any distinct bay. The mouth of the Maumee, 243 m. SSW of Detroit, is 33 m. wide, with 7 ft. water on the bar. On the S side, 42 m. E of the W end of the lake, Sandusky bay presents a considerable indentation, with shores little raised above water-level. Otter creek is navigable 40 m. by boats of 20 tons. The longest river on the N side is the Ouse, 35 m. from the NE end of the lake. It is about 200 yds. wide at its mouth. The principal rivers on the S shore are the Maumee, Sandusky, Cuyahoga, Grand River, and Buffalo Creek. The Ouse, or Welland, which unites with its E extremity, is its principal affluent, and has been taken advantage of for the construction of the Welland canal, of which it forms a part, connecting the lakes E. and Ontario, and avoiding the falls of Niagara.—The Erie canal, 370 m. in length, runs from the town of Buffalo to the Hudson river; while the Ohio canal, 334 m. in length, extends from Cleveland, at the mouth of the Cuyahoga to the Scioto, a little S of Columbus. The former of these canals places Lake E. in communication with the Atlantic; the latter connects it with the gulf of Florida.—A railway of 7 ft. gauge is now nearly completed between Dunkirk on Lake E. and New York on the Hudson river; and another railway line runs almost parallel to it from Buffalo to Albany.—Buffalo, Dunkirk, Ashtabula, Erie, Cleveland, Sandusky, Portland, and Detroit, are the principal towns on Lake E., within the territories of the U.S.; and Port-Talbot, Dover, and Sherbrooke in those of Great Britain. The trade on Lake E. is immense. Its aggregate exports and imports were estimated to amount to 115,785,000 dollars in 1848. The navigable route from Lake E. to tide-water at Montreal is performed by the Welland canal, which descends by successive locks and basins, to Port Dalhousie in Lake Ontario, and thence to Kingston, shooting the rapids, or using the other canals, in from 3 to 3\frac{1}{2} days. See article BUFFALO. "Lake E. has few of the fascinations of scenery to boast of, apart from the large mass of waters it exhibits. It is only at its W end that it is adorned with islands. The morasses, earthy scaurs, or gentle uplands of its coasts, are only remarkable for their large walnut and buttonwood trees, which, in a dense umbrageous belt, shut out all view of the interior from the traveller on the lake, except at the partial clearances. Neither is the vicinity of this lake agreeable as a residence, in the W half, at least in the summer. The heat then, although not thermometrically extreme, is peculiarly oppressive, relaxing, and long-continued. The steaming swamps-which are almost universal—are full of putrifying substances, occasioning bilious remittents. The water in common use is heated and ill-tasted. Moskitoes, sandflies and black-flies abound; and, extending their attacks to the domestic animals, and aided by a fly nearly an inch long, almost drive them distracted. On the other hand, its extreme fertility, the moderate sum of its annual heat, and its facilities of communication with other regions, will, in progress of time, render it the seat of a dense pop., and a principal granary of the W continent. Wheat, maize, and tobacco, are cultivated around it with equal success. The last named article has been grown in considerable quantity about the river Detroit, near the head of the lake; and, favoured, in a small remission of duty, by government, is sent to England, after having undergone an inland carriage to Quebec, of 814 m. Salt springs exist in almost every township, accompanied, in one or two cases, by large beds of gypsum. Bog iron ore is common on the NE side of the lake, and is worked." [Dr. Bigsby.]
ERIEUX, a river of France, which rises in the Cevennes; runs through the dep. of Ardèche, passing Chaylard; and flows into the Rhone at Beauchastel, after a course of 40 m.
ERIMO-KASTRO, a small town of Greece, 10 m. W by S of Thebes, situated on the E declivity of Mount Zagara or Helicon, and corresponding in position to the ancient Thespia.
ERINGHAUSEN, a village of Prussian Westphalia, in the co. of Mark, circle and 4 m. ESE of Lippstadt. It has manufactures of hardware.
ERIPAAL, a town of Ava, 6 m. SE of Munnipore.
ERISKAY, one of the Hebrides, separated from the island of S. Uist by a narrow strait called Eriskay-sound. It is about 5 m. in circumf.

ERITH, a village and parish of England, in Kent, on the r. bank of the Thames, 2 m. E of Woolwich. Area of p. 3,860 acres. Pop. 2,082.—The North Kent branch of the South-Eastern railway has a station at E., 13 m. 64 chains from London-bridge station; and 2 m. beyond E. passes over the river Cray upon two lofty viaducts.
ERITH, an islet in the Kent group off the SE coast of Australia, separated from Deal island by Murray pass, a channel a 1 m. wide.
ERITONGA. See HUTT.

ERIVAN, a khannate of Russian Armenia, situated between the parallels of 39° and 41° N, and the meridians of 39° and 42° E. It is bounded on the N by the cantons of Shurageli, Bambakhi, Kazakh, Shamshadilo, and Kartman, the three last of which form the circle called by the Russians Elisabetpol. A lofty chain of mountains, running in a NW and SE direction, and dividing the waters flowing N to the Kur from those flowing S to the Araxes, separates it from those districts. To the SE its frontier is divided by the course of the Little Arpa-chai from the territory of Nakshivan. To the S the Araxes forms part of the boundary, to a point directly S of Artaxata, whence it runs SW to the Little Ararat, and thence NW along the Ararat chain to the Sinek-Dagh. This chain and the Great or Western Arpa-chai, form the boundary on the side of the * ERI Turkish pashalik of Kars. The high chain of mountains which extends along the N frontier of the province, has two general denominations. Its principal peaks, from W to E, are the Ala-gos or Arakads, the Karabogas, the Shah-Dagh, the Kokurt- Dagh, and the Kongar-Dagh.—Lake Gok-shai, Sewanga, or Kegham-see, is situated on the NE part of the prov.; and is surrounded on all sides by lofty mountains. Upon one of its islands is the convent of Sewanga, or Wo-wank. This lake is 45 m. long, and towards its S end upwards of 20 m. broad. Its water is sweet; but the Turks who dwell in the neighbourhood assert that it is unwholesome, and neither use it for drinking nor cooking food. The Gok-shai, and the rivers which fall into it, such as the Balykshi, the Tokluju, and the Aghrija, abound in fish. The Araxes is joined by the Khassag, the Zenghi or Senghey, and the Little Arpa-chai. The sources of the latter are in the mountains of Nakshivan. The Zenghi joins the Gok-shai to the Araxes. It is a large stream; but as it is not navigable, is of little advantage to the country which it traverses. The other rivers of this prov. are the Korob-shai, the Adiyaman, the Kizilwank-shai, and some others which do not deserve particular notice. All of them abound in fish, but in summer are fordable: even the Araxes may then be crossed in some places.—A dark and fertile soil prevails throughout most of the cantons between Erivan and Lake Gokshai, on the borders of the Zenghi. The soil between the Araxes and Mount Ararat, and the chain to the W, is saline. Calcareous rocks, limestone, and sometimes unctuous earth, constitute the soil at the foot of the mountains.—It is computed that in the prov. of E. there are at least 22,330 houses, which would give a pop. of 113,000 of both sexes. The proportion of the pop. to the whole extent of the country may be stated at 8 persons to a square verst. If, however, we consider the mountains, marshes, and other physical inconveniences of the country, and even the mode of life of the nomades —of which there are not more than about 16,000— Klaproth is of opinion we may reckon that one-half of the superficies of this prov. is uninhabited. The pop. may be divided into two classes—the permanent class, who have fixed dwellings in the town of Erivan and the villages, and are almost all employed in agriculture—and the nomadic, who wander about the country with their flocks. The nomades amounted in 1820 to 3,153 families; and they were of four tribes, Kurds, Seljuk Turks, Kara-kapaks, and Bohemians or Gypsies. The arts practised by the inhabitants of Russian Armenia are principally tanning of leather, soap-making, and the manufacture of silk and cotton stuffs. These articles are either disposed of on the spot, or exported to Persia, Turkey, and Georgia. The transit-trade is rather in favour of this prov. To the states just mentioned Erivan exports salt, cotton, rice, tobacco, wheat, hemp, barley, flax-seed, and every kind of pulse. The nomade tribes traffic in cattle, and conduct the caravan trade. The breeding of cattle is the chief occupation of the people of this country, and the real source of their wealth. Generally speaking, they have few buffaloes, but numerous cattle, sheep, goats, and horses. The breeding of cattle is also highly necessary to that portion of the agricultural pop. which have permanent abodes; for they are obliged to attach to their unwieldy ploughs no less than 8 pairs of buffaloes or oxen. An important product of the flocks is wool; but the natives rarely shear their flocks twice a-year, as they are pastured all the year round, in summer on the mountains, and in winter in the low country, where they remain in the open air; and during severe winters-which are not rare in the environs of E.—the sheep would die were they deprived of their thick wool. There is greatly more sterile than cultivable soil in this khannate. The former occurs principally on the mountains, where, for two-thirds of the year, the climate is inimical to vegetation; and there is also much on the banks of the Araxes, where saline earth abounds. These districts are occupied by nomades, who live there in winter under tents, except those who have fixed kishlaks or winter-dwellings, who live in villages, pasturing their flocks in the neighbourhood of the Araxes. In the month of May, with the first breath of spring, the natives quit their subterranean dwellings, pack up all their property, and laying it, as well as their tents, upon the backs of their camels, oxen, and cows, ascend, day after day, higher and higher up the mountains, whose summits are covered with perpetual snow. In autumn, after the first snow falls, the fields are crowded with immense numbers of hares, which do much injury to the fruit trees by feeding upon the bark. One of the chief amusements of the people is to hunt them with dogs. In the deep marshes on the banks of the Araxes, and of the W. Arpa-chai, beavers are said to be found. In the thinly-peopled parts of E. there are numbers of brown and red bears, hyænas, wolves of uncommon size, foxes, polecats, otters, and white squirrels, resembling in colour those of Siberia. In winter the beasts of prey are so bold that they occasionally enter the towns and villages.-Russian Armenia is not particularly rich in forests; yet the cantons of Abaran, Sudaghian, Darachitchak, the S slopes of the Gokshai, Daralaos, the upper part of the valley of Garni and of the Wedi-shai, and the W part of the Great Ararat, produce wood enough for firewood. The trees consist, for the most part, of birch, linden, oak, walnut, wild apple and pear, medlar, and juniper. There are no pines; these are obtained from the pashalik of Kars, and are floated in rafts down the Araxes. The whole plain on both banks of the Araxes, as well as the uninhabited spots, is covered with bushes, amongst which occur the wild plum, the wild rose, the raspberry, barberry, and jasmine, the last of which is used for making pipes for smoking. In the low plains are found the vine, the herb called ilghin, used for making brooms, and the cholgan, the ashes of which yield potass. The natives use the latter in the form of soap, to bleach their cotton fabrics. Among dye-plants are the saw-wort and wild madder. Another plant worthy of notice occurs here. It resembles liquorice, and grows abundantly in warm and stony places. It does not rise more than a foot above the soil, in thick roundish tufts covered with long spines; on its principal branches lumps of gum form, of the size of an egg, which might be successfully employed as a substitute for gum-arabic in silkmanufacture. The khannate is subdivided into the districts of Zenghibasar, containing the cap., Sardarabad, to the NW of Erivan, Gotshak lying around the Gokshai lake, Sharur to the SE of Erivan, and Shurmali lying to the SW. See article ARMENIA. ERIVAN, IRVAN, or IRIVAN, formerly the cap. of the Persian prov. of Irivan, now the cap. of Russian Armenia, situated on the NE side of the great Araxes plain, at an alt. of rather more than 3,000 ft. above the level of the Black sea, on the river Zenghi, which rises from the Gokshai lake 70 m. further N, and falls into the Araxes 9 m. below the city. It is surrounded by barren hills, but has a fine view of the majestic Ararat in the distance. The streets are narrow and dirty, and the houses are rudely built of irregular stones cemented by a mortar of mud and straw. On one side the city surmounts a precipice 600 ft. high ERL overhanging the river, and is encompassed by a double wall. It is commanded by a spacious castle, built on a steep rock, about a 1 m. from the town, wherein the khan or governor was accustomed to reside, which is surrounded by a triple mud wall on three sides, and is inaccessible on the fourth. This fort, however, is itself commanded from all the adjoining hills. [Wilbraham.] The immediate environs of E. are naturally barren, but by unceasing toil and diligent irrigation, have been made very productive. It was remarked by the travellers of last cent., that E. "abounds more in vineyards and gardens than in houses." Its mosques have beautiful gardens attached to them, and the place is still famous for its fruit, especially melons. It has suffered severely from repeated sieges, which have reduced it to a ruinous condition, and greatly diminished the pop.; and it is still represented as falling into decay. Klaproth estimated the pop. in 1829 at 14,000; and the number of houses at 2,731. E. was 30 years ago, a place of some commercial importance. The great caravan-line from Poti and Redout-Kaleh to Tauris, touched upon it, and its caravanserais were generally filled with crowds of traders; but the caravans now avoid the Russian territory, and the most considerable merchants have left the place.-In 1635, E. having been taken from the Turks by the king of Persia, its position was changed to its present site, about 1 m. from where it stood formerly. The pasha of Diarbekir sat down before it in 1724, with a numerous army, of which he lost 24,000, in four unsuccessful attempts to storm it; but the inhabitants, after a brave defence, at length capitulated. It was taken by Nadir Shah in 1748, and since his time has been in possession of the Persians. The Russians blockaded the city during six months in 1808, and then endeavoured to take it by assault, but were repulsed with great slaughter, and lost half their army in its subsequent retreat to Teffis. It was taken, however, by them in 1827; and their possession of it was confirmed by treaty with Persia the following year.—The climate of E. is trying to those accustomed to an equable climate, for while the heat of summer is excessive, the cold of winter is equally so.—Wagner.—Wilbraham.
ERKELENZ, a town of Rhenish Prussia, 25 m. NE of Aix-la-Chapelle. Pop. 2,046 in 1837. The town was formerly fortified.

ERKENE, or Erghene, a large river of Turkey, in the prov. of Romelia, which rises in several headstreams on the W flank of the Stanches-Dagh or Little Balkan; flows SW through the sanjak of Viza, and turning NW enters that of Gallipoli; and joins the Maritza, on the r. bank, 22 m. S of Adrianople, after a course of about 90 m.
ERKHEIM, a village of Bavaria, in the circle of Suabia, on the Günz, near Memmingen. Pop. 1,600.
ERKSDORF, a village of Hesse-Cassel, in the circle of Kirkchain. Pop. 646. The French were here defeated by the Allies on 16th July, 1760.
ERL, a stream of Bavaria, in the Pfalz circle, which joins the Otter, and flows into the Rhine.

ERLA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 30 m. NNW of Saragossa, on the r. bank of the Arva-de- Biel.—Also a town of Russia, in the gov. of Livonia, district of Wenden.—Also a small stream of Lower Austria, which flows into the Danube on the r. bank, SE of Ens.
ERLABRUNN, a village of Franconia, on the Maine, with 800 inhabitants, 4 m. N of Schainfeld.
ERLACH, a small but well-built town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Bern, at the foot of the Jolimont, on the S bank of the lake of Bienne, 20 m. WNW of Bern. Pop. 540.

ERLAF (GREAT and LITTLE), two rivers of Austria, which rise on the mountains that border on Styria, and uniting near Wieselburg, fall into the They are both rapid Danube near Pöchlarn. streams, and unfit for navigation.

ERLANGEN, or CHRISTIAN'S ERLANGEN, a town of Bavaria, cap. of a bail. of the same name, in the circle of Central Franconia, situated in a well-cultistanz. vated plain, in N lat. 49° 35′ 36″, E long. 11° 4′, 23 m. S of Bamberg, near the confluence of the Regnitz and Schwabach. It is divided into the old and new town, the latter of which was founded by Christian Ernest, margrave of Bayreuth, in 1686, and was first inhabited by French refugees: hence the name, Christian's E. It is surrounded by walls, and has 7 gates. The New town is handsome, and regularly built. Among the public edifices are 3 Lutheran and 2 Reformed Lutheran churches, a Roman Catholie church, an orphan asylum, an infirmary, and a military hospital. The palace, which was partially destroyed by fire in 1814, has been fitted up for the use of the Protestant university, founded by Frederick of Bayreuth in 1743. The establishments connected with it are a clinical school, an hospital, an equestrian academy, a gymnasium, an ecclesiastical seminary, a polytechnic school, an academy of fine arts, a chemical laboratory, a botanical garden, a philological seminary, an anatomical school, museums of natural history, and experimental philosophy, and a library of upwards of 100,000 vols. It is attended by from 250 to 300 students. The Leopold-Caroline academy of naturalists, established in 1666 simultaneously with the Parisian academy of sciences, has its seat here, and is the oldest institution of the class in Germany.—The pop. of the town in 1847 was 9,268, of whom only 382 were Roman Catholics.—E. has factories for weaving and printing cotton goods, and manufactories of linen, stockings, and hats, gloves, leather, tobacco, plate glass, and toys.-E., together with the principality of Bayreuth, became an appendage of the Bavarian crown by the treaty of 1809. ERLAU, EGER, or JAGER, a town of Hungary, in the com. of Heves, situated in a pleasant valley watered by a rivulet of the same name, an affluent of the Theiss, 67 m. NE of Buda, in N lat. 47° 53' 54". The pop., in number about 20,000, is of mixed descent, Germans, Hungarians, and Rascians. E. is the see of an archb. whose suffragans are the bishops of Szathmar, Rozenau, Kaschau, and Zips. It has several good public edifices, particularly the cathedral and the archbishop's palace. The citadel is strong and stands on a lofty rock. A considerable quantity of red wine is grown in the environs.—E. suffered frequently in the Turkish wars; and in 1800 no less than a third part of it was consumed by fire.
ERLBACH, a town of Bavaria, in Middle Franconia, 17 m. W of Nuremberg. Pop. 970.
ERLBACH (UNTER), a town of Bavaria, in Middle Franconia, 4 m. W of Spalt.
ERLEBACH, a river of Germany, which rises in Nassau, and runs into the Nidda 2 m. W of Wilbel.

ERLENBACH, or ALBE, a small village of France, in the dep. of Bas-Rhin, 2 m. NNW of Schelestat. Pop. 1,088. On the heights near this v. was fought an obstinate action between the French and Prussians on 29th Nov. 1793.—Also a large and wellbuilt village of Switzerland, 18 m. SSE of Bern, on the l. bank of the Simmen river. Pop. 1,242.—Also a village of Wurtemberg, in the Neckar circle, on the Salm. Pop. 1,071.
ERLENBAD, a village of Baden, in the circle of the Middle Rhine, 6 m. E of Achern.
ERLIBACH, or EHRLIBACH, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. and 4 m. S of Zurich. Pop. 915.
ERLINGHEIM, a village of Wurtemberg, 9 m. NW of Ludwigsburg. ERN
ERLINSBACH, a parish of Switzerland, in the cant. of Soleure, on the Aar. Pop. 748.
ERMACH, a small town of Austria, in Carinthia, 9 m. S of Saxenburg.
ERMATINGEN, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Thurgau, on the Unter-see, 3 m. W of Con- Pop. 1,584. A considerable quantity of wine is grown in the environs.
ERME (SAINT), a parish of Cornwall, 4 m. NNE of Truro. Area 3,780 acres. Pop. 552.

ERMELAND, an ancient division of Poland, comprising the modern Prussian circles of Braunsberg, Heilsberg, Rossel, and Allenstein, in the regency of Königsberg; and giving title to a bishop whose seat is at Frauenburg.
ERMELO, a village of Holland, in Guelderland, 3 m. S of Harderwyk. Pop. 1,100.
ERMENAK, a town of Asiatic Turkey, in the pash. of Itchil, 63 m. S of Konieh, on the Gheuk-su. Some remains of the ancient Homonada exist here.

ERMENONVILLE, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of the Oise, 7 m. SE of Senlis, on an affluent of the Nonette, with a castle and gardens. Rousseau lies interred in a grove of poplars in the park belonging to the castle.
ERME-OUTRE (SAINT), a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Aisne, cant. of Sissonne. Pop. 1,834.
ERMESLEBEN, or Ermsleben, a town of Prussian Saxony, in the regency and 38 m. NW of Merseburg, on the Selke. Pop. 2,299 in 1837.
ERMETON-SUR-BIERT, a commune and village of Belgium, in the prov. and 9 m. SW of Namur. Pop. 425.
ERMINGTON, a parish in Devon, 2 m. NW by N of Modbury. Area 4,920 acres. Pop. in 1801, 917; in 1841, 1,607.
ERMS, a small river of Wurtemberg, which falls into the Neckar 4 m. SW of Nurtingen.-Also a small river of Devonshire, which rises in Dartmoor, and flows into the English channel 5 m. SSW of Modbury.
ERMUA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 34 m. E of Bilbao.
ERNATIA, a town of Asiatic Turkey, on the gulf of Satalia, in the sanj. and 16 m. NW of Satalia.

ERNE, a fine river, and two magnificent lakes, the former chiefly, and the latter almost wholly, in co. Fermanagh. The river issues from Lough Ganny or Gawna, on the border of the cos. of Longford and Cavan, and runs N through the latter co. till it becomes lost in the begun expansion of Upper Lough E. From the foot of Lower Lough E. to Donegal bay it has a W run of 8 m., and makes a splendid cataract at Ballyshannon. Its chief accessions of volume are from a series of rivulets which drain the whole of co. Fermanagh, and parts of the contiguous cos., and fall into the lakes. The river is navigable to Ballyshannon for vessels drawing 12 ft. of water; but it is so rapid as to be not at all navigable between Ballyshannon and Belleek. Upper Lough E., the middle or connecting reach of the river E., and Lower Lough E., unitedly extend NNW from end to end of co. Fermanagh, and very nearly along its middle. Though the Upper lake measures 8½ m. in extreme length, by <math>3\frac{3}{4}</math> in extreme breadth, and the Lower lake 12½ by 5½ m., both are so profusely studded with islands, and intricately indented with peninsulæ, that each great section of the entire valley appears from almost every vantage-ground like a continuous labyrinth of land and water. Mr. Inglis, a good judge, and keen observer, but who chanced to see these lakes under very favourable auspices of weather, says: "I confidently assert that Lower Lough E., take it all in all, is the most beautiful lake in the three kingdoms; and but for the majestic Alpine outline that bounds the horizon on the upper part of Lake Leman,-Lake Leman itself could not contend in beauty with this little-visited lake in the co. of Fermanagh." The Lower lake has an alt. above the level of low-water spring-tide in Donegal bay, of 148 ft. in summer, and of from 154 to 156 ft. in winter. It varies much in depth, from only a foot or a yard to 200 or 230 ft. The Upper lake has a mean alt. above the Lower lake's level of 20 inches, but ranges between 11 and 34 inches; and it rarely exceeds 20 ft., and nowhere 75 ft. in depth. The two lakes, their connecting reach of river, and several slow-running streams which flow into them, constitute by far the greatest extent of natural navigation in Ireland; and have been opened to the NE of the kingdom by the Ulster canal. The area of the Upper lake is 9,453 acres; that of the Lower lake is 27,645 acres.
ERNE'E, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Mayenne, 17 m. NNW of Laval, on a stream of the same name, which flows into the Mayenne 2 m. N of Laval. Pop. 3,577.
ERNESTOWN, a township in the Midland district of Upper Canada, touching on the S on Lake Ontario. Pop. 4,317.
ERNOLSHEIM, a commune of France, in the dep. of Bas-Rhin, cant. of Molsheim. Pop. 767.
ERNSBACH, a town of Wurtemberg, in the Jaxt circle, 8 m. NE of Ohringen, on the Kocher. Pop. 863. It has extensive iron-works.
ERNSDORF, a large village of Prussian Silesia, in the circle and 2 m. NW of Reichenbach. Pop. 1,600.
ERNSTBRUNN, a town of Lower Austria, in the circle and 10 m. N of Korneuburg. Pop. 821.
ERNSTHAL, a small town of Saxony, in the co. of Schonburg, 7 m. W of Chemnitz. Pop. 2,700. It has active cotton manufactures.

ERNUINGASH, a posting-station of Russia, in the gov. of Nijegorod, near the frontiers of Kasan, on the route from Vasil to Bieloi-Vrag. It is a collection of low wooden huts, inhabited by Cheremisses, who form the pop. of the surrounding district.
EROEN (CAPE), a promontory on the S coast of the Japanese island of Jesso, in N lat. 41° 55'.
EROME, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Drome, cant. of Fain. Pop. 1,923. It has extensive potteries.
EROPHIVO, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Irkutsk, 60 m. N of Balaganskoi.

EROUAD, or Erroad, a fortified town of India, in the prov. of Coimbatur, 58 m. NE of Coimbatur, on the Colerun river, in N lat. 11° 21'. It originally belonged to the naich of Madura, but was taken from him by the rajah of Seringapatam in 1667. It was taken by the British in 1768; retaken by Hyder Ali in the same year; again taken by the British in 1790; and retaken by Tippu. Along with the prov. it finally came into the possession of the British in 1799. It has a small district around it bearing the same name; and the town at one period contained 3,000 houses, but during the various sieges it has experienced, has been greatly reduced.
EROUCHETI, a town of Russian Armenia, 22 m. S of Akhalzike, and 85 m. W of Erivan.
ERP, a large village of Holland, in N. Brabant, 3 m. E of Vechel, and 8 m. N of Helmont.
ERPE, a river of Hesse-Cassel, which falls into the Dymel opposite Warburg.—Also a commune of Belgium, in E. Flanders, 8 m. SW of Termonde.
ERPFINGEN, a town of Wurtemberg, in the Schwarzwald circle, 9 m. S of Reutlingen. Pop. 782.
ERPINGHAM, a co. of Norfolk, 3 m. N of Aylesham. Area 1,350 acres. Pop. 475. ERR

ERQUELINES, or ERQUELINNES, a commune and v. of Belgium, in the prov. of Hainault, arrond. and 20 m. SW of Charleroi, on the Sambre. Pop. 480.—The Charleroi and E. railroad, now approaching its completion, forms an important link in the railway communication from Paris to Germany, the route by Creil, St. Quentin, E., Charleroi, Namur, Liege, and Cologne, being 105 kilometres or 65 m. shorter than the route by Valenciennes, Brussels, and Malines. It will likewise shorten the route from Paris to Brussels by 45 kil. or 30 m.
ERQUI (CAPE), a promontory on the coast of France, in the dep. of Cotes-du-Nord, in N lat. 48° 35'.
ERQUINGHEM-LYS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Armentières. Pop. 2,053.
ERQUY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Cotes-du-Nord, cant. of Pleneuf. Pop. 1,951.
ERR, a commune of France, in the dep. of Pyrenées-Orientales, cant. of Saillagoute. Pop. 734.
ERRA, a town of Portugal, on the river Erra, 22 m. SE of Santarem.—The river runs into the Zatas, 12 m. E of Salvatierra, in the prov. of Estremadura.
ERRABARI, a village of Egypt, 10 m. NW of Cairo.
ERREEF. See RIF.
ERRIGAL, a parish of co. Londonderry, 5 m. WNW of Kilrea. Area 19,625 acres. Pop. 5,748.
ERRIGAL-KEROGUE, a parish in co. Tyrone, on the Blackwater. Area 21,139 acres. Pop. 9,171.
ERRIGAL-TROUGH, a parish in cos. Tyrone and Monaghan. Area 24,793 acres. Pop. 9,585.
ERRINGDEN, a parish of England, in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 7 m. W of Halifax. Pop. 2,221.

ERRIS, a highland and maritime district in the NW of co. Mayo, bounded, on the W and N, by the Atlantic; on the E, by the barony of Tyrawley; and on the S, by the barony of Burrishoole. Its greatest length, from N to S, is 27 m.; its greatest breadth on the mainland, 171 m.; its greatest breadth, including islands and intervening sounds, 211 m.; and its area 232,889 acres. What is popularly termed the district of E. includes also all the contiguous waste and mountainous portions of Tyrawley and Burrishoole; and measures about 30½ by 23½ m.; and is bounded, on the E, by a line drawn from Downpatrick-head to the Lough-Conn base of Nephin,— on the SE, by a line from the base of Nephin to Burrishoole-abbey; and on the S, by Clew-bay.
ERRIS-HEAD, a cape on the W side of the entrance of Broadhaven, in co. Mayo, 43 m. N of Belmullet.
ERRO, a river of Italy, which runs into the Bormida, 3 m. SW of Acqui.
